title: The Crucial Challenge of Building Space Expertise
gist: FIU president Jeanette Nuñez argues Latin America's binding constraint in the space race is workforce and institutional capacity, not access to hardware, and that the U.S. can outcompete China's infrastructure-plus-financing model by pairing universities with government to build durable local expertise.

themes.theme: From hardware access to institutional capacity

insights.claim: Nuñez's central thesis is that Latin American countries are no longer just seeking satellites or launch services but the institutional and technical capacity to participate meaningfully in the space economy, meaning the region increasingly seeks partnership, not dependency.
insights.speaker: Jeanette Nuñez
insights.so_what: Reframes the competitive battleground from hardware sales to capacity-building services — vendors offering only satellites/launch without training and technology transfer will lose out.
insights.quote: In short, the region increasingly seeks partnership, not dependency.
insights.locator: That ambition is reshaping international cooperation

insights.claim: Falling launch costs, wider technology availability, expanding low Earth orbit satellite networks, and rising strategic value of geospatial data have lowered barriers to entry, shifting the real challenge from acquiring space technology to developing the expertise to govern, integrate, and benefit from it long-term.
insights.speaker: Jeanette Nuñez
insights.so_what: As hardware barriers fall, the differentiator shifts to human capital — a durable moat for whichever partner builds local talent pipelines first.
insights.locator: Broader changes in the global space economy are accelerating this shift

insights.claim: Shortages of engineers, geospatial analysts, cybersecurity specialists, and space policy professionals remain major barriers to Latin America building homegrown space capabilities.
insights.speaker: Jeanette Nuñez
insights.so_what: Names the specific talent categories in shortage — a concrete target list for education/training investment or recruiting in the region's space sector.
insights.locator: Across much of the region, shortages of engineers

insights.claim: China has expanded its regional footprint not just via infrastructure but by pairing satellite cooperation with financing, technical training, and research partnerships, while Russia has pursued its own initiatives including establishing a GLONASS ground station (its GPS equivalent) in Venezuela.
insights.speaker: Jeanette Nuñez
insights.so_what: China's bundled financing-plus-training model is more comprehensive than a pure hardware sale — the U.S. needs to match this bundling, not just compete on satellites alone.
insights.locator: The demand for a space-ready workforce also presents an opportunity

insights.claim: Nuñez argues the U.S. can remain the region's preferred space partner only by extending beyond traditional security cooperation to prioritize education, workforce development, research collaboration, technology transfer, and public-private partnerships that build local capacity.
insights.speaker: Jeanette Nuñez
insights.so_what: A prescriptive roadmap for U.S. space diplomacy — success requires soft-power/capacity-building investment, not just hardware or defense deals.
insights.locator: The U.S. can remain the preferred partner

insights.claim: Nuñez positions U.S. universities like Florida International University as uniquely able to train the engineers, policymakers, entrepreneurs, scientists and legal experts who sustain national space ecosystems while serving as hubs for international collaboration.
insights.speaker: Jeanette Nuñez
insights.so_what: Universities are framed as strategic soft-power assets in the space-geopolitics competition, not just education providers — relevant for assessing FIU-style institutional partnerships as a channel for U.S. influence.

insights.claim: Nuñez's closing prediction is that the future of space cooperation in the Americas will depend not on who launches the most satellites but on who helps build the people and institutions capable of using them effectively — the partner that invests in those foundations will be the partner that endures.
insights.speaker: Jeanette Nuñez
insights.so_what: A direct call to judge U.S.-China space competition in Latin America by workforce/institution investment rather than launch counts or satellite numbers.
insights.quote: The future of space cooperation in the Americas will depend not on who launches the most satellites, but on who helps build the people and institutions capable of using them effectively.
insights.locator: The future of space cooperation in the Americas
